coredata.dc:description: In the present study, the influences of the carbon quantum dots nanoparticle addition to diesel- microwave-assisted corn oil biodiesel-dimethyl carbonate fuel mixtures on combustion, performance and emissions were experimentally researched in a DI diesel engine at 4.12, 9.61, 15.10 and 20.60 Nm. Lower in-cylinder pressure was measured using fuel mixtures according to diesel. The rise of carbon quantum dot fraction in the fuel mixtures lead to decrease ID. It was also found that SFC declined with the addition of carbon quantum dot nanoparticle in the fuel blends. ITE increased by 6.66 % using CF QD120 ppm according to CF at 15.10 Nm. CD was shortened with fuel mixtures compared to diesel. Test results also showed that clear increase on MPRR and RI has been realized with fuel mixtures. Higher CO was measured using fuel mixtues. Nevertheless, the addition of carbon quantum dot to CF decreased CO emissions. CO declined by about 14.12 % with CF QD120 ppm according to CF at 20.60 Nm. Significant decrease was measured using carbon quantum dot addition on soot emissions whereas NOx emissions increased. Soot emissions reduced by 19 % with CF QD120 ppm compared to CF at 20.6 Nm.

row.entry:
Diesel, corn oil biodiesel,
dimethyl carbonate,
carbon quantum dot

With increase in engine load; CD, MPRR, RI, CO, CO2, NOx and soot increased while COVimep and HC decreased. With the use of composite fuel; COVimep, MPRR, RI, CO, CO2, SFC and NOx increased while CD, HC and soot decreased. With the increase in quantum dots; CD, COVimep, MPRR, RI, CO2 and NOx increased, while CO, HC, soot and SFC decreased.

With increasing engine speed; BP and NOx increased and HC decreased. The best BSFC and BTE were obtained at the maximum torque point. With increasing biodiesel ratio; BP, BTE and NOx increased, BSFC and HC decreased.

Diesel, waste fish oil biodiesel,
quantum dot

With increasing engine speed; BP, CO, CO2, BSFC, UHC and NOx increased while ET and BSFC decreased. With increasing biodiesel ratio; CO2, NOx increased, ET, BP, BSFC, CO and UHC decreased. With the addition of QD; ET, BP, CO and CO2 increased, while BSFC, UHC and NOx decreased.

With increasing engine speed; BP, BSFC, CO2, UHC and NOx increased while ET decreased. Biodiesel ratio; CO2 and NOx increased, ET, BP, BSFC and UHC decreased. With the increase in ethanol, quantum dot ratio; ET, BP, CO2 and NOx increased, BSFC, UHC and NOx decreased.

Diesel, canola oil biodiesel,
quantum dot

With increase in engine load; CD, CA50, ITE, MPRR, RI, CO, CO2, HC, NOx and soot increased while SFC and COVimep decreased. With the use of biodiesel; SFC, COVimep, MPRR, RI, CO, CO2 and NOx increased, while CD, CA50, ITE, HC and soot decreased. With Quantum dots; CD, ITE, COVimep, MPRR, RI, CO and NOx increased, SFC, CA50, CO2, HC and soot decreased.
With increase in engine load; HC, NOx and smoke increased while ID decreased. With the use of biodiesel; HC, NOx and CO increased, smoke and ID decreased. No stable change was observed in pressure.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In the present study, the influences of the carbon quantum dots nanoparticle addition to diesel- microwave-assisted corn oil biodiesel-dimethyl carbonate fuel mixtures on combustion, performance and emissions were experimentally researched in a DI diesel engine at 4.12, 9.61, 15.10 and 20.60 Nm. Lower in-cylinder pressure was measured using fuel mixtures according to diesel. The rise of carbon quantum dot fraction in the fuel mixtures lead to decrease ID. It was also found that SFC declined with the addition of carbon quantum dot nanoparticle in the fuel blends. ITE increased by 6.66 % using CF QD120 ppm according to CF at 15.10 Nm. CD was shortened with fuel mixtures compared to diesel. Test results also showed that clear increase on MPRR and RI has been realized with fuel mixtures. Higher CO was measured using fuel mixtues. Nevertheless, the addition of carbon quantum dot to CF decreased CO emissions. CO declined by about 14.12 % with CF QD120 ppm according to CF at 20.60 Nm. Significant decrease was measured using carbon quantum dot addition on soot emissions whereas NOx emissions increased. Soot emissions reduced by 19 % with CF QD120 ppm compared to CF at 20.6 Nm.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:def-description.ce:para: Crank angle location of 50 % accumulated HRR (°CA)
Rubber seed oil methyl ester-diesel blend

ce:section.ce:para: The increase in transportation population brings with it the problem of greenhouse gas and climate change. Therefore, it is necessary to improve the current methods used in transportation. Reduction of harmful gases and improve the efficiency are two main challenges on internal combustion engines (ICE). To overcome these difficulties alternative fuel sources and various additives have been utilized for ICE especially in diesel engines owing to higher thermal efficiency and better fuel economy. Among the alternative solutions usage of biodiesel comes first owing to higher oxygen content and cetane number. Nevertheless, poor pulverization characteristics, higher density and viscosity of biodiesel make the usage of biodiesel difficult [1–5]. In the present case, the improvement of biodiesel properties is seen critical to obtain higher efficiency. Microwave method presents less reaction time and improvement on fuel properties compared that transesterification method [6–9]. On the other hand, dimethyl carbonate (DMC) has a major usage potential as an additive to biodiesel/diesel mixture because it is nontoxic, mixable, environment friendly. Furthermore, it has lower boiling point, carbon/oxygen rate and higher hydrogen/carbon rate [9–13]. So, DMC is seen to be a challenging candidate as an additive not only improve the biodiesel/diesel mixture but also reducing the detrimental exhaust gases. Apart from these, nanoparticle supports to improve the bad properties of biodiesel that is crucial due to pulverization and combustion. The increase of oxidation ratio becomes possible in the existence of nanoparticle with biodiesel mixtures [14–18]. Nanoparticles can provide significant improvements in combustion, performance and emission values due to their small energy carriers in blended fuels [19]. Examples of nanoparticles used in the literature for this purpose are graphene oxide [20], aluminum oxides, titanium oxide, carbon nanotubes [21], copper oxide, graphene nanoplates, zinc oxide [22], quantum dots [23]. Significant improvements can be achieved in engine combustion, performance and emission values with the use of quantum dots. The thermal, chemical and mechanical effects of the nanoparticles used against the engine parts are also of great importance. Quantum dots have dimensions of 2–10 nm and are liquid [24]. Quantum dots can improve the thermal conductivity of the mixed liquid [25]. When these properties of Quantum dots are evaluated, it is seen that they do not have a harmful effect on the engine parts. Taheri-Garavand et al. [1] predicted the performance and emissions via using artifical neural network (ANN) with diesel–biodiesel mixtures including quantum dot. Brake power and engine torque increased with the addition of GQD+E (graphene quantum dot + ethanol) to 10 %(waste fish oil biodiesel) WOFB. Besides, the addition of GQD+E to 10 % WOFB caused to decrease brake specific fuel consumption (BSFC), carbon monoxide (CO), unburned hydrocarbon (UHC) and nitrogen oxide (NO). Kumar et al. [26] observed the lubrication performance of carbon nanomaterials. They reported that carbon dot-based lubricant additives performed reducing characteristics of for friction. Joshi et al. [25] studies the effects of amine treated graphene quantum dots in vapor compression refrigeration system. It has been found that thermal conductivity of polyalkylene glycol lubricant oil was improved in case amine treated graphene quantum dots was used. Opuz et al. [15] investigated the influences of metallic fuel with canola oil biodiesel on combustion, performance and emissions. The addition of metallic fuel caused to decrease ignition delay (ID). SFC declined by about 17.69 %, 8.70 % and 3.08 % using D80TCB20C150 D80TCB20C100, D80TCB20C50 and according to D80TCB20 at 15.10 Nm. Remarkable decrease was measured on soot emissions using metallic fuel with D80TCB20. Zhang et al. [23] researched the carbon quantum dots co-production and biofuels with hydrothermal conversion. Great attention has been paid for hydrothermal conversion in view of the carbon quantum dots production. Razzag et al. [27] examined the influences of dimethyl carbonate (DMC) and biodiesel mixture on combustion, performance and emissions. They have demostrated that B30 + DMC, B20 + DMC and B10 + DMC presented higher brake power by 0.95 %, 1.22 % and 1.70 % respectively without antioxidant. CO reduced by about 26.47 %, 25 % and 19.04 % using B30 + DMC, B20 + DMC and B10 + DMC respectively accordibg to biodiesel mixtures without DMC. Heat release rate raised by about 10.40 %, 10.67 % and 5.52 % for B30 + DMC, B20 + DMC and B10 + DMC compared that B30, B20 and B10. Ramesh et al. [11] studied the influences of DMC and exhaust gas recirculation (EGR) in a diesel engine operated with diesel and Prosopis juliflora biodiesel mixture. It was pointed out that EGR and DMC cuased to reduce smoke and nitrogen oxide (NOx) but HC and CO raised. It was realized that the best values were obtained on BSFC, brake thermal efficiency (BTE) and exhaust gas temperature by 0.27 kg/kWh, 32.93 % and 310.89 °C with 20 % EGR ratio and DMC (5 ml). EL-Seesy et al. [28] investigated the effects of waste cooking oil biodiesel (WCO) on engine performance and exhaust emissions. To improve the combustion characteristics of WCO biodiesel, n-decanol was added and functional MWCNTs (with nitrogen and amino) were added to the blend. The results showed that the WCO biodiesel-decanol blend was close to diesel fuel in engine performance, but was successful in reducing NOx and soot emissions. The addition of functional MWCNTs resulted in even more significant reductions in NOx, CO and soot emissions. Gülüm [29] mixed the gasoline and dimethyl carbonate into diesel and corn oil mixture. He analyzed the influences of combustion and performance. He found that maximum in-cylinder pressure declined by 4.0457–6.2025 % and 4.6701–7.3418 % with gasoline mixtures and dimethyl carbonate mixtures respectively according to diesel. In addition maximum heat release rate declined 0.4–1.2654 % and 0.8020–4.5627 % with gasoline mixtures and dimethyl carbonate mixtures respectively. Sentilkumar and Rajan [30] made an experimental study to determine the influences of DMC with biodiesel-diesel mixture (RSME25) in view of emission and performance evaluation. Ignition delay (ID) was prolonged via DMC fumigation according to RSME25 and diesel at full load. It was mentioned that better emission and performance values were taken using 30 % fumigation with RSME25 compared that other test conditions. Rajan et al. [5] researched the emission and properties of corn biodiesel. They found that 67 % efficiency, 55 min duration were obtained for corn oil biodiesel while 91 % efficiency and 2.5 min duration was determined with corn oil biodiesel microwave (CBDMW). CBDMW presented less CO, HC, smoke and NO by 0.09–0.12 g/kWh, 0.09–0.25 g/kWh, 0.4–0.45 BSU and 0.8–1.5 g/kWh respectively than corn oil biodiesel. Jannatkhah et al. [31] performed a thermodynamic analysis of corn biodiesel and diesel mixture. They mentioned that BTE increased while BSFC decreased. Minimum BSFC was obtained using B30 at full load and 1700 rpm. It was also declared that maximum exergy efficiency was obtained with B10 corn biodiesel. Reddy et al. [32] discussed the influences of corn biodiesel mixtures on combustion, performance and emissions. They explored that CSME B20 showed higher thermal efficiency compared that other biodiesek mixtures. Fuel mixtures emitted higher CO and HC compared that diesel at full load. It was clearly explained that CSME B20 was a notable fuel mixture in view of being powerful alternative in case of diesel.Singh and Kaushal [33] aimed to investigate the bio-nano diesel emulsion in view of efficiency via Taguchi technique. 75, 50 and 25 ppm quantum dot have been added into biodiesel mixtures. 0.0011 % CO, 0.81 ppm HC, 317 ppm NOx, 30.36 % BTE, 294 g/kWh BSFC and 2.48 kW brake power have been found as the best values.

In this work, it was highlighted the double effect of dimethyl carbonate and nanoparticle (carbon quantum dot) in a diesel engine fueled with diesel/corn oil biodiesel mixture. Detailed consideration has been performed on combustion, performance and emissions using diesel/biodiesel fuel mixture with dimethyl carbonate and nanoparticle addition. The trend of changes of combustion characteristics, engine performance, CO, CO2, HC NOx and soot emissions have been experimentally investigated.

In this study, the investigation of composite and nanocomposite fuels depending on engine load was carried out. The design of the experimental including the definition, design and run experiment sections of the study was created. Schematic representation of the design of the experimental is given Fig. 1 .

Biodiesel was produced from corn oil by microwave-assisted transesterification reaction. A modified household-type microwave was used in the experiments. In the microwave-assisted tranesterification reaction, NaOH (0.5 wt%) was used as the catalyst and methyl alcohol (25 wt%) was used as the alcohol. The reaction was carried out at 100 W microwave output power, 500 rpm stirring speed, 60 °C temperature and 10 min reaction time.

70 % diesel, 20 % corn oil biodiesel and 10 % dimethyl carbonate were mixed and base fuel was obtained (CF). Carbon quantum dot was obtained from the market (Sigma Aldrich) and used in the experiments. 40, 80 and 120 ppm carbon quantum dot nanoparticle were added into obtained base fuel. Fuels with 40, 80 and 120 ppm additives are named CF QD 40 ppm, CF QD 80 ppm and CF QD 120 ppm, respectively. All fuel mixtures were weighed for targeted mass and volumetric concentrations. The weighed fuel mixtures were homogenized in an ultrasonic bath for 30 min. The schematic representation of the apparatus in which the fuel mixtures were prepared is given in Fig. 2
[34].
Test fuel properties are given in Table 1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Engine performance, combustion and emissions characteristics
ce:section.ce:para: This paper aims to research the effects of carbon quantum dots nanoparticle in biodiesel-dimethyl carbonate fuel mixture. Experimental setup was established at Afyon Kocatepe University, Technology Faculty in Internal Combustion Engine Laboratory as seen in Fig. 3 . For tests, a single cylinder Antor AD510 BS diesel engine was used. The specifications of the test engine were presented in Table 2 . Regenerative dynamometer was coupled with test engine that is capable of 20HP power absorbtion. Engine loads were specified as 20.60, 15.10, 9.61 and 4.12 Nm. To eliminate the measurement differences, the test engine was heated up and data were taken. Table 2 shows the some fuel properties.

HC, CO, CO2, soot and NOx, CO, CO2, HC and soot emissions were determined using Bosch BEA 350 gas analyzer. AVL QC34C brand pressure sensor and Kistler brand 5018A charging amplifier were used in pressure measurements. Crankshaft position was determined with Opkon brand 1000 pulse encoder with 0.36 °C sensitivity. Pressure and crankshaft position information was transferred to computer environment with National instrument 6351 model data collector. Technical specifications and percentage uncertainty values of exhaust gas analyzer and measurement devices are given in Table 3 .

The percentage uncertainty (PU) values given in Table 3 were calculated using Eq. (1) and the total uncertainty value was calculated using Eq. (2) [1,35]. The total uncertainty (TU) value was calculated as 0.72 %.

In-cylinder pressure data were collected with AVL QC34C in-cylinder pressure sensor. In-cylinder pressure data were taken with the sensitivity of 0.36 °CA. Kistler 5018A amplifier amplified the pressure data. Analog data that were taken from in-cylinder pressure have been converted to digital data via using National instrument 6210 data acqusition card. Moreover TFA ST-490 infrared thermometer was used to measure the temperature of the engine block. Eq. (3). was used to calculate the heat release rate based on the first law of thermodynamics. Eq. (4) was utilized to determine the heat transfer to cylinder wall [36–47].

The influences of nanoparticle on cylinder pressure and heat release rate were examined. Fig. 4 depicts the in-cylinder pressure trace and heat release rate variations versus crank angle. In-cylinder pressure raised with the increase engine load for each test fuel.Maximum in-cylinder pressure was obtained with diesel for each engine load. In-cylinder pressure declined with fuel mixtures owing to lower heating value. The addition of carbon quantum dot nanoparticle additive caused to increase in-cylinder pressure. Higher activation energy of nanoparticle caused to improve chemical oxidation reactions. It can be also mentioned that the fraction of nanoparticle addition leads to increase in-cylinder pressure [46–55]. Two stage heat release called premixed and diffusion combustion is seen in compression ignition engines as seen in Fig. 4. It can be said that nanoparticle addition caused to advance combustion. Higher chemical reactivitiy of nanoparticle improved the oxidation reactions [42,47–61]. In fact, there is no big differences on cylinder pressure between fuel mixtures. However, it may be explained that CF QD80ppm and CF QD40ppm showed reasonable performance among the fuel mixtures especially at high engine loads. Furthermore, it is clear to mention that heat release rate is retarded using carbon quantum dot addition into CF as seen in Fig. 4. The addition of DMC and nanoparticle showed the better effect at high engine loads. High gas temperature and pressure accelerated the oxidation reactions at high engine loads. Chemical oxidation reactions are positively affected by the combined effect of carbon quantum dot and dimethyl carbonate addition.

ID reflects the position where the first flame kernel was formed after injection. The ID result of this study is given in Fig. 5 . Even if biodiesel has higher cetane number, higher density and viscosity of biodiesel caused to obtain longer ID. Oxygen and fuel molecules could not be well mixed because of the poor atomization characterisitics of biodiesel. This situation retards to physical fragmentation, evaporation and forming of first flame kernel. In addition, it can be demonstrated that low cetane number of dimethyl carbonate is the other reason of longer ID. It was reported that the addition of nanoparticle provided the shorter ID. Carbon quantum dot nanoparticle addition accelerated the oxidation reactions with thermal and radiation properties. The shortest ID was specified with CF QD120ppm at 20.60 Nm [48,50–53,58–60].

Fig. 6 represents the variation of SFC. Higher density and viscosity of biodiesel caused to inject higher fuel fraction by mass into the cylinder. Moreover, heating value of fuel blends are getting lower compared to diesel. So more fuel should be ignited to generate same engine power with fuel blends. One of the most significant outcome of Fig. 6 is that nanoparticle addition decreased the SFC. It can be emphasized that higher oxygen content of biodiesel and nanoparticle improved oxidation reactions. Furthermore, carbon quantum dot nanoparticle improved the thermophysical properties of test fuel and increased the flame speed. So, SFC decreased with the addition of nanoparticle [42,48,53–55,60–70]. Minimum SFC was detemined at 15.10 Nm for all test fuels. SFC decreased by about 2.58 %, 3.23 % and 2.91 % with CF QD40ppm, CF QD80ppm and CF QD120ppm compared to CF respectively at 15.10 Nm.

CA50 defines the position where the half of fuel mixture was completed to combust versus crank angle. There is close relationship between ITE and CA50. CA50 should be after TDC for high thermal efficiency [42–46,70–72]. Diesel showed the longest CA50 according to other test fuels. Oxygen content of biodiesel and dimethyl carbonate caused to obtain shorter CA50. It was also noticed that nanoparticle addition provided to decrease CA50 as seen in Fig. 7 -a. Nanoparticle material addition improved the combustion characteristics. It also provides fast combustion phasing. Fig. 7-b shows the variation of ITE according to engine load. It was realized that maximum ITE was calculated at 15.10 Nm with diesel. The addition of microwave assisted corn biodiesel and dimethyl carbonate caused to decrease ITE. However, ITE increased with the addition of nanoparticle carbon quantum dot. ITE increased by about 4.73 %, 6.02 %, and 6.66 % with CF QD40ppm, CF QD80ppm and CF QD120ppm compared to CF respectively at 15.10 Nm.

Fig. 8 shows the CD values with test fuels according to engine load. The highest CD was determined with diesel. It can be mentioned that CD decreased with the addition of biodiesel and dimethyl carbonate according to neat diesel. On the other hand, nanoparticle additive caused to decrease CD. Nanoparticle additive increases the raction rate resulting in shorter CD. Moreover, nanoparticle additive acts as catalyst and energy carrier during oxidation reactions. So it accelerates the combustion phasing. It can be contributed that the addition of DMC and quantum dot caused to accelerate oxidation reactions. It can be also emphasized that combustion is completed earlier with DMC and nanoparticle addition into CF. Poor injection properties of biodiesel is improved and chemical reactions are accelerated with the nanoparticle addition.

Cyclic variations show the durability and stability of the operation of test engine. Cyclic variations are seen depending on the fuel properties, temperature, pressure and thermodynamic situation of the charge mixture during compression stroke. Fig. 9 explains the changing of COVimep. It was seen that the lowest COVimep was computed with diesel. COVimep declined with the rise of engine load. Combustion conditions are improved owing to higher in-cylinder gas temperature and variations are reduced cycle by cycle. Chemical reactions are accelerated and COVimep increased with fuel blends. It can be also concluded that the addition of nanoparticle caused to increase COVimep. For stable operation, COVimep should not be exceeded %10 in ICE [42–46]. COVimep exceeded 10 % with CF QD80ppm and CF QD120ppm test fuels 4.12 and 9.61 Nm engine loads as seen in Fig. 9. At 20.60 Nm engine load, COVimep was computed as 4.54 %, 7.556 %, 8.257 %, 9.539 %and 9.572 % with diesel, CF, CFQD 40 ppm, CFQD 80 ppm and CFQD 120 ppm respectively.

Fig. 10 -a shows the MPRR variation according to engine load with test fuels. It was found that MPRR increased with the rise of engine load for all test fuels. Higher amount of charge mixture resulted in higher pressure at the end of combustion. Lower MPRR was observed with diesel compared that fuel blends. It was also realized that nanoparticle additive caused to obtain higher MPRR. MPRR raised with the rise of nanoparticle addition in fuel mixtures. The highest MPRR was determined with CFQD 120 ppm test fuel at 20.60 Nm. It can be said that biodiesel and dimethyl carbonate provided faster vaporization. It causes faster combustion phasing. Dimetyhl carbonate also supports to provide better atomization and air/fuel mixing. In addition to these, nanoparticle carbon quantum dot enhances the chemical reactivation resulting in higher in-cylinder pressure. Fig. 10-b depicts the variation of RI with test fuels. Similar trend was observed with RI like MPRR. The rise of engine load leads to increase RI. Higher oxygen content and activation energy of nanoparticle improved the oxidation reactions and knocking tendency increased [18,42–46,71–84].

CO is incomplete combustion product from internal combustion engines. CO can be formed absence of enough oxygen and temperature during combustion. Fig. 11 gives the variation of CO versus engine load. The rise of engine load resulted in higher CO as seen in Fig. 11-a. It can be explained that higher fuel molecule concentration weakens the oxidation reactions because of insufficient oxygen. Hence, CO is released. Moreover, the addition of biodiesel caused to emit higher CO compared to diesel. In case nanoparticle is added to CF, CO is reduced [18,44–53,71–84]. CO reduced by about 2.60 %, 6.69 %, and 14.12 % with CFQD 40 ppm, CFQD 80 ppm and CFQD 120 ppm according to CF respectively at 20.60 Nm. The addition of nanoparticle acted as a catalyst accelerating and improving combustion. As seen in Fig. 11-b, CO2 increased with the rise of engine load like CO. Higher oxygen content of biodiesel and nanoparticle additive caused to emit higher CO2. So, it can be said that there is a harmonious exchange between CO and CO2. Nanoparticle additive increases the reaction rate resulting in higher CO2. CO2 increased by about 5.31 %, 5.90 %, and 6.16 % with CFQD 40 ppm, CFQD 80 ppm and CFQD 120 ppm according to CF respectively at 20.60 Nm.

Flame front is extinguished when it reaches to the cooler cylinder wall and HC is formed in the combustion chamber. HC is also produced, if oxygen and fuel molecules could not react each other in piston and piston ring cavities owing to insufficient homogeneity. Fig. 12 depicts the variation of HC according to engine load. HC increased with the rise of engine load. Lower amount of oxygen concentration prevents to complete combustion. Thus, HC is formed. Biodiesel addition showed higher HC compared to diesel because of poor injection characteristics and obtaining homogeneous charge. Nevertheless, the addition of dimetyhl carbonate and nanoparticle reduced HC. It can be stated that nanoparticle additive enhanced to form lower HC, because carbon deposits can be ignited due to higher activation energy of nanoparticles. HC reduced by 4.61 %, 7.69 % and 12.30 % with CFQD 40 ppm, CFQD 80 ppm and CFQD 120 ppm according to CF respectively at 20.60 Nm.

Fig. 13 presents the variation of NOx with test fuels. It can be clearly seen from Fig. 13-a that NOx increased with the rise of engine load for all test fuels. The reason for this change observed in NOx emissions is the increase in in-cylinder temperature depending on the engine load [85]. Biodiesel and dimetyhl carbonate provides oxygen content and higher reaction rate resulting in higher in-cylinder gas temperature during combustion. So, nitrogen and oxygen molecules can react well each other. Hence NOx emissions is formed. Similar increases in NOx were observed with the use of biodiesel, with similar details found in the literature [35]. Combustion characteristics are developed and better ignition is provided with nanoparticle additive. NOx increased by about 23.82 %, 25.20 % and 25.90 % with CFQD 40 ppm, CFQD 80 ppm and CFQD 120 ppm according to CF respectively at 20.60 Nm. Local fuel mixture zones and inhomogeneity of charge mixture cause to form soot emissions in diesel engines. Fig. 13-b represents the changing of soot emissions according to engine load. Higher charge mixture tends to increase soot formation as seen in Fig. 13-b. But, nanoparticle additive leads to reduce soot emission. The increase of nanoparticle addition caused to prevent soot in fuel blends espacially with CFQD 80 ppm and CFQD 120 ppm test fuels. Fuel atomization should be improved with nanoparticle additive and faster vaporization is performed. This situation enhances the improving of chemical oxidation reactions. In addition, higher activation energy of nanoparticle accelerates the oxidation reactions. It was also provided that poor properties of biodiesel were improved with nanoparticle and dimetyhl carbonate. It helps to obtain lower amount of soot. It can be also noticed that radiation and thermal properties of nanoparticles caused to occur micro explosion resulting in better oxidation reactions [42–53].

The results of the studies containing diesel, biodiesel, dimethyl carbonate and carbon quantum dots in the literature and the results obtained in this study are given in Table 4 . When the studies are examined, it is determined that NOx increase occurs with the use of biodiesel and the increase in this study is normal. It was observed that the composite fuel formed with diesel, corn oil biodiesel, dimethyl carbonate and carbon quantum dots provided significant improvements in the soot parameter. It was observed that the use of carbon quantum dots caused improvements in CO and HC emissions. Opuz et al. [15] observed similar improvements in HC and soot emissions with the use of canola oil biodiesel and quantum dots. Reddy et al. [32] observed a similar increase in NOx emissions with the addition of corn oil biodiesel to diesel fuel. Maleni et al. [86] obtained improvements in BSFC and NOx emissions by adding ethanol and quantum dot to diesel and biodiesel fuel blends. However, the ethanol addition is thought to be the main reason for these improvements. An increase in SFC and a decrease in ITE were observed with the addition of biodiesel to diesel fuel. These disadvantages caused by the use of biodiesel are eliminated by the use of quantum dots. The use of quantum dots provided significant improvements in SFC and ITE values. Garavand et al. [1] achieved similar improvements in BSFC value with the use of quantum dots.

In this study, the effects of microwave corn biodiesel, dimethyl carbonate and carbon quantum dot nanoparticle addition to diesel fuel on combustion, performance and exhaust emissions were investigated. Firstly, the improvement on the fuel characteristics of pure diesel was aimed by microwave biodiesel addition. In the second step, dimethyl carbonate was added as an additive. Finally, carbon quantum dot was added to the diesel–biodiesel-dimethyl carbonate fuel mixture. When the fuel blends were used, the in-cylinder pressure decreased compared to diesel fuel. The lowest ID was determined with CFQD 120 ppm. The findings showed that SFC decreased by 2.91 % with CFQD 120 ppm compared to CF at 15.1 Nm engine load. Compared to the CF at 15.1 Nm, a 6.66 % increase in thermal efficiency was obtained with CFQD 120 ppm. With the use of biodiesel, the negatively oriented SFC and ITE values were improved by the use of carbon quantum dot nanoparticles. The test results also showed that the cyclic changes increased with fuel blends compared to diesel. On the other hand, a significant increase in MPRR and RI was detected by using biodiesel, dimethyl carbonate and nanoparticles compared to diesel. CO decreased by 14.12 % compared to the pressure of CF at 120 ppm at 20.6 Nm. HC decreased by 12.30 % at 20.6 Nm with CF QD 120 ppm, while NOx increased by 25.9 % with CFQD 120 ppm. A sharp decrease in soot emissions was observed with fuel blends compared to diesel. Significant reductions in HC and CO emissions were determined with the addition of carbon quantum dot to blended fuels. It was concluded that the combined effect of microwave biodiesel, dimethyl carbonate and nanoparticle showed a better effect on combustion and performance in DI diesel engine than diesel.
